section.title: Thanksgiving for Their Faith
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with a warm greeting from Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy, then bursts into thanksgiving for a young church that received the gospel in power, endured suffering with joy, and became a model to whole regions. Their turning from idols to serve the living God and to await His Son frames the whole letter.
section.thesis: Paul thanks God for a chosen people whose faith, love, and hope prove the gospel came to them in power, who became imitators of the Lord in suffering and a beacon to others, having turned from idols to serve the living God and to wait for the Son who rescues from the coming wrath.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting; thanksgiving for their work of faith, labor of love, and endurance of hope; and the gospel's overflow as their example rings out and their conversion is described.

units.label: Greeting
units.note: grace and peace to a young church

verses.text: Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy, To the {{ekklesia|church}} of the Thessalonians in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ: {{charis|Grace}} and peace to you.

units.label: Thanksgiving for faith, love, and hope
units.note: the triad that proves the gospel came in power

verses.text: We always thank God for all of you, remembering you in our prayers
and continually recalling before our God and Father your work of {{pistis|faith}}, your labor of {{agape|love}}, and your enduring {{elpis|hope}} in our Lord Jesus Christ.
Brothers who are beloved by God, we know that He has {{eklegomai|chosen}} you,
because our {{euangelion|gospel}} came to you not only in word, but also in power, in the Holy Spirit, and with great conviction, just as you know we lived among you for your sake.
And you became {{mimetes|imitators}} of us and of the Lord when you welcomed the message with the joy of the Holy Spirit, in spite of your great suffering.

units.label: The gospel overflows
units.note: an example that rings out, a people who turned to serve and to wait

verses.text: As a result, you have become an example to all the believers in Macedonia and Achaia.
For not only did the message of the Lord ring out from you to Macedonia and Achaia, but your faith in God has gone out to every place, so that we have no need to say anything more.
For they themselves report what kind of welcome you gave us, and how you turned to God from {{eidolon|idols}} to serve the living and true God
and to await His Son from heaven, whom He raised from the dead, Jesus our {{rhyomai|deliverer}} from the coming {{orge|wrath}}.

ekklesia.gloss: An assembly or congregation, the gathered people of God. Paul addresses not a building or an institution but the church of the Thessalonians, and locates them in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. The word underlines that even this new, small, persecuted group is a true assembly belonging to God.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor and kindness. Paul opens with grace and peace, a signature Christian greeting that weds the Greek word for favor to the Hebrew shalom. It sets the tone of the whole letter: everything the Thessalonians are and have flows first from God's grace in Christ.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, and faithfulness toward God. Paul praises their work of faith, faith that is not idle but active and visible. It is the first member of the triad faith, love, and hope, and the theme the whole letter returns to as evidence that God has truly worked among them.

agape.gloss: Self-giving love, the love God pours out and calls His people to show. Paul commends their labor of love, love that costs effort and toil. It is the second member of the triad and, later in the letter, something Paul prays will increase and overflow toward one another and toward all.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation of what God has promised, not wishful thinking. Their enduring hope in the Lord Jesus produces steadfastness under suffering. This forward look to Christ's return is the third member of the triad and the beating heart of the letter's later teaching on the coming of the Lord.

eklegomai.gloss: To choose or elect. Paul says he knows God has chosen these believers, and his evidence is the gospel's power and their transformed lives. Election here is a pastoral comfort: their faith, love, and hope are the visible fruit of God's prior, gracious choice of them.

euangelion.gloss: The gospel, the good news of what God has done in Jesus Christ. Paul stresses that this gospel came not merely in words but in power, in the Holy Spirit, and with deep conviction. The message itself, carried by the Spirit, is what created the church and changed everything.

mimetes.gloss: An imitator, one who follows a pattern. The Thessalonians became imitators of Paul and of the Lord by receiving the word with joy even amid affliction. Imitation of Christ, especially His pattern of joy in suffering, marks them out and then spills over as they become an example to others.

eidolon.gloss: An idol, a false god or image. Their conversion is described as turning from idols to serve the living and true God. In a pagan city full of shrines, this decisive turning captures the essence of repentance: leaving dead idols to serve the God who is alive.

rhyomai.gloss: To rescue, deliver, or draw out of danger. Jesus is named as the one who delivers His people from the coming wrath. The word points to real rescue from real judgment, and it frames the whole letter's hope: the Son they await is the Savior who snatches them from wrath.

orge.gloss: Wrath, God's settled and righteous judgment against sin. The coming wrath is the judgment from which Jesus rescues His people. Far from a loss of temper, it is God's just response to evil, and Paul returns to it later to assure believers they are not appointed to wrath but to salvation.

basic.context: Paul, along with Silas and Timothy, writes to a young church he had planted in Thessalonica and then had to leave quickly under pressure. This opening is one long outburst of thanks to God for them. Their faith, love, and hope are so real and so visible that news of them has spread everywhere.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a warm, genuine thanksgiving, not flattery. Paul is describing what God actually did among them, so we should read it both as a record of a real church and as a picture of what the gospel does wherever it truly lands: it changes people, produces faith, love, and hope, and turns them from idols to the living God.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul praises their work of faith (pistis), labor of love (agape), and endurance of hope (elpis), a famous triad. The good news is the euangelion, the gospel, which came in power. They became mimetes, imitators, of the Lord, and turned from eidolon, idols, to await Jesus, their rhyomai, deliverer, from the coming orge, wrath.
basic.typology: Turning from idols to serve the living God echoes Israel being called out of a world of false gods to worship the one true God. Awaiting God's Son from heaven takes up the Old Testament hope of God coming to save His people, now focused on the risen Jesus.
basic.christ: Jesus is the risen Son we wait for from heaven and the deliverer who rescues us from the coming wrath. The gospel about Him came in power by the Spirit, and imitating Him, even in joyful suffering, is what marks His people.
basic.god: God is the living and true God, over against dead idols. He chooses and loves His people, He sends the gospel in power by His Spirit, and He raised Jesus from the dead. He is a God who saves, and who will one day judge, but who rescues His own from that judgment.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 13 lifts up the same triad of faith, hope, and love. Acts 17 tells how the church in Thessalonica was born amid opposition. And Romans 5 speaks of being saved from God's wrath through Jesus.
basic.oneStory: God has always called a people out of idolatry to serve Him and to wait for His saving intervention. Here that story reaches a young Greek city, where the gospel creates a church that turns from idols to the living God and waits for the risen Son, the deliverer from wrath.

context.literary: The letter opens with a brief tri-part greeting, then an extended thanksgiving that runs from verse 2 through the end of the chapter and beyond. Paul stacks up the marks of a genuine work of God: the triad of faith, love, and hope; the gospel in power; joyful endurance; and a witness that has spread far. It functions as an overture, sounding themes the rest of the letter develops.
context.historical: Thessalonica was a major, prosperous port city and the capital of Macedonia, full of pagan cults and imperial religion. According to Acts 17, Paul preached in the synagogue for three Sabbaths, saw many turn, and was then driven out by a mob within weeks. This letter, written soon after from Corinth around AD 50 or 51, is likely among the earliest of Paul's letters, addressed to a church that is new and under pressure.
context.canonical: As one of the earliest New Testament documents, this thanksgiving gives an unusually early window into apostolic preaching and its effect. The triad of faith, love, and hope becomes a fixed Christian summary, and the closing note about awaiting the Son and rescue from wrath sets up the letter's later teaching on the coming of the Lord.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure a young, suffering church of God's electing love by rehearsing the clear evidence of the gospel's power among them, and to anchor their identity in serving the living God and awaiting His Son.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage mainly describes what God did in Thessalonica, yet it holds up an enduring pattern: genuine conversion shows itself in faith, love, and hope, in joyful endurance of suffering, and in turning from idols to serve and to wait. What is described of them is meant to be recognizable in every true church.

debates.question: How should we understand Paul's confidence that God has chosen them?

views.label: Election grounds the assurance
views.case: Paul knows they are chosen because God's grace came first; the power of the gospel and their changed lives are the visible fruit and proof of that prior choice.

views.label: Changed lives are the evidence, not the basis
views.case: Some stress that Paul infers election from observable fruit, so the emphasis falls on the reality of transformed lives as the ground of pastoral assurance, without speculation about hidden decrees.

debates.bearing: Either way, assurance rests not on feelings but on the gospel's power producing real faith, love, and hope, all traced back to God's gracious initiative.

debates.question: What is the coming wrath from which Jesus delivers?

views.label: God's final judgment
views.case: The wrath is God's just, future judgment on sin at the last day, from which Jesus rescues His people, as Paul develops in chapter five.

views.label: Judgment already breaking in
views.case: Some see wrath as both a present reality against sin and a future certainty, so deliverance is both a present rescue and a sure hope.

debates.bearing: In both readings Jesus is the rescuer from real judgment, and awaiting Him is a hope grounded in His resurrection.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the thanksgiving is the triad: work of pistis, faith; labor of agape, love; endurance of elpis, hope. Each noun is paired with a term of effort, so faith works, love labors, and hope endures. Paul knows God has chosen (eklegomai) them because the euangelion, gospel, came in power and by the Holy Spirit. They became mimetes, imitators, of the Lord, receiving the word with joy amid affliction. Their conversion is summed up as turning from eidolon, idols, to serve the living and true God and to await His Son, Jesus, the rhyomai, the one who rescues, from the coming orge, the settled wrath of God against sin.

typology.type: Turning from idols to the living God
typology.fulfillment: Takes up Israel's call to abandon false gods, now fulfilled as Gentiles turn to the true God through the gospel of His Son.
The Old Testament hope of God coming to save is focused on the risen Jesus, who will return to rescue His people from wrath.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center of the thanksgiving: the church exists in Him, their hope is in Him, and their whole conversion is oriented to Him. He is the Son whom God raised from the dead and whom they await from heaven. Above all He is named the deliverer, the one who rescues His people from the coming wrath. The gospel that is about Him came in power by the Spirit, and imitating Him, particularly His pattern of joy in the midst of suffering, becomes the visible signature of those who belong to Him.
study.god: God the Father is named alongside the Lord Jesus at the very start, the source of grace and peace. He is the living and true God, sharply contrasted with the dead idols the Thessalonians abandoned. He loves and chooses His people, He sends the gospel in power by His Holy Spirit, and He raised Jesus from the dead. He is both the God who saves now and the God whose righteous wrath is coming, yet He has provided in His Son the rescue from that very judgment.

crossReferences.note: Faith, hope, and love abide as the enduring marks of the Christian life, the same triad Paul praises here.
The founding of the Thessalonian church amid opposition, the background to this letter.
Believers are saved from God's wrath through Jesus, echoing the deliverer from the coming wrath.
God has not appointed us to wrath but to salvation, developing the hope introduced in verse 10.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has called a people out of idolatry to serve Him and to wait for His saving intervention. Here that long story reaches a young Greek city, where the gospel of the risen Son creates a church marked by faith, love, and hope. They turn from idols to the living and true God and lift their eyes to heaven, awaiting Jesus, the deliverer from wrath. Their story is a fresh instance of God gathering a people for Himself, and their forward-looking hope points to the day the whole story is heading toward.

sermon.title: Hope Will Put You to Work: 1 Thessalonians 1:2-7, Part 1
sermon.why: In this Look at the Book study, Piper works through the opening thanksgiving, showing how the triad of faith, love, and hope drives real work, labor, and endurance, and why hope in Christ energizes the Christian life. A careful, orthodox treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: What three things does Paul give thanks for in the Thessalonians?

options.text: Their work of faith, labor of love, and endurance of hope.
options.note: Verse 3 names the famous triad of faith, love, and hope.

options.text: Their wealth, size, and social standing in the city.
options.note: Paul commends spiritual fruit, not status or numbers.

options.text: Their impressive buildings and organization.
options.note: The church was new and under pressure; Paul praises faith, love, and hope.

options.text: Their careful keeping of Jewish festivals.
options.note: These were Gentiles who turned from idols to the living God, not festival-keepers.

quiz.q: How did the gospel come to the Thessalonians?

options.text: Not only in word, but in power, in the Holy Spirit, and with deep conviction.
options.note: Verse 5 stresses the gospel came with Spirit-given power and conviction.

options.text: Only as clever human arguments.
options.note: Words were involved, but the power was the Holy Spirit's, not mere rhetoric.

options.text: Through signs alone, without any message.
options.note: The message came in word, yet accompanied by power and the Spirit.

options.text: By force and compulsion.
options.note: They welcomed the message with joy; it was received, not forced.

quiz.q: How is the Thessalonians' conversion summed up in verses 9 and 10?

options.text: They turned from idols to serve the living God and to await His Son from heaven.
options.note: Verses 9-10 give this threefold summary: turned, serve, await.

options.text: They kept their idols but added Jesus alongside them.
options.note: They turned from idols; there was a decisive break, not an addition.

options.text: They stopped believing in any god at all.
options.note: They turned to the living and true God, not away from all faith.

options.text: They simply changed their moral behavior with no change of allegiance.
options.note: The turning was a change of worship and allegiance to God through His Son.

quiz.q: How is Jesus described in relation to the coming wrath?

options.text: As the deliverer who rescues His people from the coming wrath.
options.note: Verse 10 names Jesus our deliverer from the coming wrath.

options.text: As the one who ignores the coming wrath.
options.note: The wrath is real and coming; Jesus rescues from it rather than ignoring it.

options.text: As the cause of wrath against His people.
options.note: Jesus is the rescuer from wrath, not its source against His own.

options.text: As someone unrelated to judgment.
options.note: Jesus is precisely the deliverer from the coming judgment.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say he knows God has chosen the Thessalonians?

options.text: Because the gospel came in power and produced real, changed lives.
options.note: Verses 4-6 tie the assurance of election to the gospel's power and their fruit.

options.text: Because they were naturally more religious than others.
options.note: The ground is God's work in them, not their natural religiosity.

options.text: Because they had earned God's favor by their efforts.
options.note: Their fruit flows from God's grace and choice, not from earning favor.

options.text: Because they were wealthy and successful.
options.note: Election is shown by faith, love, and hope, not by prosperity.

questions: Paul saw faith that works, love that labors, and hope that endures. Which of these three is God most stirring you to grow in right now?
The Thessalonians received the word with joy even in the middle of suffering. Where do you need the Spirit's joy to help you hold on to God's word under pressure?
They turned from idols to serve the living God. What functional idols still compete for your allegiance, and what would it look like to turn from them afresh?
Their faith became known everywhere without them trying to publicize it. How might a genuinely changed life speak the gospel to those around you this week?
Jesus is described as the deliverer from the coming wrath. How does resting in that rescue change the way you face the future?

translationNotes.note: Silvanus is the fuller form of the name Silas, Paul's companion in Acts. The BSB footnote notes this.
Where the BSB prints an em dash before just as you know, house style renders it as a comma: with great conviction, just as you know.
Where the BSB prints an em dash after from the dead, house style renders it as a comma: raised from the dead, Jesus our deliverer from the coming wrath.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Thessalonians 1:1-10. Em dashes in verses 5 and 10 rendered as commas per house style.
